title: Advise Clients to Make a Business Interest Gift When Preparing for Sale
summary_raw: It’s the most overlooked high-impact charitable resource.

summary_ai.digest: Advisors should coach clients to gift business interests to charity before a sale to unlock tax deductions equal to the full sale proceeds, not just the gifted stake—a strategy most wealth managers miss despite its high impact for founders and business owners.
summary_ai.summary_long: The article highlights a tax-optimization tactic for founders and business owners approaching a liquidity event: gifting an ownership stake to a charitable donor-advised fund (DAF) or qualified charity before a sale triggers a charitable deduction based on the anticipated sale price of the entire business, not just the gifted portion. This is possible because the gift is valued at fair market value pre-sale, and the charity is treated as a co-seller in the transaction. The deduction can be substantial—potentially in the millions for mid-market and larger exits—yet remains underutilized by wealth advisors. The strategy works best when timed before a sale closes and requires coordination between tax counsel, the business advisor, and the wealth manager. For RIAs and wealth platforms, positioning this capability signals sophistication and can deepen client relationships during high-impact liquidity events.
talking_points: A founder can gift 10-20% of a business to a DAF, receive a charitable deduction based on the full business valuation, then execute the sale—deferring and potentially eliminating tax on a meaningful portion of proceeds.

The deduction size depends on the business valuation and the gifted percentage; a $50M sale with a 15% gift unlocks a $7.5M deduction, worth $2-3M in federal tax savings depending on the seller's bracket.
This requires pre-sale execution and coordination across tax advisors, M&A counsel, and the wealth manager—a friction point that makes it a differentiator for organized practices.
summary_ai.highlight: Gifting a business stake to charity before a sale generates a deduction based on the full enterprise valuation, not just the gifted portion—a high-six to seven-figure tax benefit most advisors never surface.

title: Long-Short SMAs Aim to Keep the Tax Losses Flowing
summary_raw:
It’s become harder for direct-indexing strategies to generate the tax losses that underpin much of SMAs’ appeal.

The post Long-Short SMAs Aim to Keep the Tax Losses Flowing appeared first on The Daily Upside.

summary_ai.digest: Direct-indexing SMAs are losing their tax-loss harvesting edge as markets rise; wealth managers are pivoting to long-short strategies to sustain the tax-alpha narrative that drives client fees.
summary_ai.summary_long: Direct-indexing strategies built their value proposition on consistent tax-loss harvesting—offsetting gains in a diversified portfolio by selling underwater positions. But sustained market gains have shrunk the pool of losers available to harvest, eroding the tax-alpha advantage that justifies SMA fees to high-net-worth clients. Wealth managers are now experimenting with long-short overlays (short hedges on indices while long on individual stocks) to artificially manufacture tax losses in a rally, keeping the tax story alive. This is both a business risk (if long-short doesn't work, the tax premise collapses) and a sign of how fee-dependent the direct-indexing pitch has become.
talking_points: Direct-indexing's core selling point—tax-loss harvesting—breaks down in bull markets; rising AUM in SMAs now forces the industry to engineer losses rather than find them.
Long-short overlays are essentially a fee-preservation play: if they generate alpha, great; if they just manufacture tax losses, the client still wins—but it's a fragile model if returns disappoint.
This is a warning sign for RIA SMA platforms: as competition commoditizes direct-indexing and markets normalize, the tax-alpha moat erodes fast.
summary_ai.highlight: Tax-loss harvesting—the engine of SMA fee justification—stops working in rallies; managers are now layering long-short strategies to keep the tax story alive, a tacit admission that the value prop is fragile.

summary_ai.digest: SEC proposal would grant accredited investor status to clients under advice from registered advisors, bypassing traditional net-worth or income gates. Advisor community is split on whether this expands access or creates compliance risk.
summary_ai.summary_long: The SEC is considering a rule change that would automatically classify clients as accredited investors if they work with a registered investment advisor, RIA, or broker-dealer—removing the need to meet strict income ($200k+) or net-worth ($1M+) thresholds. Proponents argue this democratizes access to private placements, hedge funds, and alternative assets for a broader middle-class investor base. Skeptics worry advisors lack incentive to police suitability, that advisors themselves may have conflicts pushing unsuitable alternatives, and that the rule conflates advisor registration with investor sophistication. The proposal touches on IRA platforms, wealth management scale, and fintech distribution channels—all areas where product democratization and regulatory friction matter.
talking_points: If passed, this rule eliminates the income gate for alternatives access—meaning a $400k household with an RIA can suddenly buy into $5M hedge funds or SPACs currently reserved for the wealthy.
RIAs and advisors become de facto gatekeepers for accreditation, raising compliance and liability exposure if clients later claim they weren't suitable for tail-risk products.

This plays directly into the fintech and platform narrative: IRA custodians and robo-advisors could theoretically onboard mass-market clients and grant them access to private-market products, reshaping the asset allocation industry.
summary_ai.highlight: If advisors become the suitability check, not net worth, the rule reshapes who can buy alternatives and how platforms compete to serve them.

title: Leveraged Stock ETFs Are as Stable as a Row of Dominoes
summary_raw: Follow the money.

summary_ai.digest: Leveraged stock ETFs face structural volatility risks during market stress, with daily rebalancing mechanics amplifying losses and creating potential contagion across asset classes.
summary_ai.summary_long: Leveraged ETFs use daily rebalancing to maintain fixed multiples (2x, 3x) of underlying indices, which forces them to sell on down days and buy on up days. During volatile periods, this mechanical process locks in losses and creates a decay drag that compounds over time. The article frames this as a systemic risk: if a sharp drawdown hits, leveraged ETFs could trigger forced selling across equities, derivatives, and potentially credit markets—a domino effect that could amplify losses for retail and institutional holders alike. The piece implies that risk officers and compliance teams at asset managers need to model tail scenarios where leveraged ETF outflows collide with other deleveraging flows.
talking_points: Leveraged ETFs decay in sideways or choppy markets due to daily rebalancing; a 20% swing down then up leaves you with less than you started with.
If equities drop 10%+ in a day, leveraged ETF rebalancing could force $billions in selling across equity and derivatives markets, cascading losses.
Wealth managers holding leveraged ETF positions for clients face tail-risk exposure they may not fully model—this is a compliance and suitability conversation waiting to happen.
summary_ai.highlight: Leveraged ETF rebalancing mechanics create a forced-seller dynamic that could trigger cascading losses across equities and derivatives during a 10%+ single-day decline.

title: ‘I’d hate to end up with an unexpected tax bill’: I’m 73 and still work full time. Can I avoid paying taxes on my Social Security benefits?
summary_raw: “I’m actually earning more each week than I ever have before.”

summary_ai.digest: 73-year-old full-time worker asks whether continued earnings trigger taxation of Social Security benefits; MarketWatch tax columnist explains the combined income thresholds and marginal tax impact.
summary_ai.summary_long: A reader earning above-market wages at age 73 while collecting Social Security worries about tax liability on benefits. The column reviews the IRS combined income test: if modified adjusted gross income plus half of Social Security benefits exceeds $25,000 (single) or $32,000 (married), up to 85% of benefits become taxable. The adviser walks through the math for a high earner and notes that continued work—especially at elevated pay—almost certainly pushes the reader into full taxation of benefits. The takeaway is that there is no practical avoidance strategy short of deferring benefits or reducing earned income.
talking_points: Combined income threshold for single filers is $25k; exceeding it by any amount triggers taxation of up to 85% of Social Security, not a partial phase-out.
A full-time worker earning 'more than ever before' at 73 will almost certainly cross both the $25k and $34k (full taxation) thresholds, eliminating any tax-avoidance room.
No legal strategy exists to shelter high earners from this tax—deferring benefits or reducing W-2/1099 income are the only practical levers.
